task: I’m looking at a used generator listing and the seller says it was “serviced recently,” but I don’t fully trust that. What are the first few things you’d want to verify on the unit or in the paperwork before believing that claim?

turns.agent_response:
I’d verify these first, in this order:

## 1) Identity
- Exact **model**
- **Serial number**
- **Hour meter**
- Fuel type
- Match all of that to the listing

If the identity is off, stop trusting the rest.

## 2) Service proof tied to hours
Ask for:
- **dated receipts/invoices**
- the **service item** listed
- the **hour reading** at service
- any technician notes

A receipt without hours is weak. A date without the unit’s hours is also weak.

## 3) Maintenance items you can see
Look at:
- oil condition
- air filter
- spark plugs if accessible
- battery terminals
- belts/hoses
- fuel condition
- signs of fresh cleaning/paint hiding wear

## 4) Start and run behavior
Check:
- cold start
- idle stability
- smoke
- noise
- warning lights
- whether it runs smoothly under load

## 5) Battery / starting system
Verify:
- battery age/date code
- terminal corrosion
- cable condition
- charger/maintainer if standby unit

A “recent service” claim means little if it cranks poorly.

## 6) If it’s standby, check ATS / transfer gear
- model/condition
- any visible damage or corrosion
- whether it transfers properly if they’ll demonstrate it

## Red flags
- no paperwork
- paperwork with no hours
- hours don’t match wear
- fresh paint over grime/rust
- weak start or unstable run
- seller won’t show a load test

## Best proof of real service
- nameplate photo
- hour meter photo
- invoice with date + hours + service items
- photo of replaced parts if claimed
- video of cold start and load test

If you want, I can turn this into a **strict check / evidence / red flag table** for the listing.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. But I’d tighten the order a bit and be more specific on what counts as proof.

First thing I’d verify is the **service record against the hour meter**. Not just “recently serviced” — I want:
- date
- hour reading at service
- what was actually done
- who did it

If they can’t tie the work to the hours, I don’t trust the claim.

Second, I’d look for **signs the machine was actually exercised after service**:
- starts clean
- holds frequency/voltage if you can test it
- no surging
- no fault history showing recent alarms

Third, for a used generator specifically, I’d want paperwork on the stuff sellers love to skip:
- battery age / replacement
- oil and filter change
- coolant service if applicable
- fuel system cleaning or stabilization
- load test results

And if it’s standby, ATS matters. If they say serviced recently but the transfer gear is corroded or never tested, that’s a problem. Same with a generator that was wiped down to look fresh but has old hoses, crusty terminals, or a dead battery.

So yeah — the key is not the phrase “serviced recently.” It’s whether the paperwork, hour meter, and physical condition all line up.

If you want, I can give you a blunt 10-point buyer checklist you can use on-site.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — I’d verify these first, in this order:

## 1) Service record tied to the hour meter
Look for:
- **date**
- **hour reading at service**
- **what was actually done**
- **who did it**

If the service record doesn’t match the hour meter, don’t trust the “recently serviced” claim.

## 2) Identity / listing match
Confirm:
- **exact model**
- **serial number**
- **fuel type**
- **hour meter reading**

If the unit doesn’t match the listing exactly, stop and re-check everything else.

## 3) Evidence it was actually exercised after service
Check for:
- clean start
- stable idle
- stable voltage/frequency if you can test it
- no surging or hunting
- no recent fault history or alarms

A generator that was “serviced” but not exercised is still a question mark.

## 4) The maintenance items sellers often skip
Ask for proof of:
- **battery age / replacement**
- **oil and filter change**
- **coolant service** if applicable
- **fuel system cleaning/stabilization**
- **load test results**

These are the parts that make the claim believable.

## 5) Standby-specific gear
If it’s standby, inspect:
- **ATS / transfer switch**
- corrosion
- signs of heat or damage
- whether it was actually tested

A clean generator with neglected transfer gear is not a clean setup.

## 6) Physical condition vs claimed service
Look for mismatch:
- old hoses
- crusty terminals
- dead or weak battery
- fresh paint hiding wear
- dirt/rust that doesn’t fit “recent service”

## What counts as real proof
Best evidence is:
- invoice with **date + hours + service items**
- hour meter photo
- serial/model photo
- load test record
- battery date code or replacement record
- before/after photos if parts were replaced

## Red flag
If the paperwork, hour meter, and physical condition don’t line up, the service claim is weak.

If you want, I can turn this into a **blunt 10-point buyer checklist** you can use on-site.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the order I’d use too.

The only thing I’d make even stricter is this: if the service record doesn’t pin to the hour meter, it’s basically marketing. Date alone is weak. Hours alone without the work details are weak. Need all three lined up.

And on the exercised part — I’d want more than “it starts.” I’d want evidence it was run under load, or at least a load-test record, because a no-load start can hide a lot.

So yeah, that’s solid. Clean sequence, good red flags, and the proof standard is right. If you want, I can still turn it into that on-site checklist, but this answer itself is basically the right filter.
